content.text: Reliability and Availability Are Different NFRs

Availability and reliability are related but distinct requirements. Availability is the percentage of time the integration is operational and accepting requests. Reliability is the probability that a request, once accepted, succeeds — a service can be available but unreliable if accepted requests frequently fail with errors.
'The integration responds to health checks and accepts requests X% of the time over a rolling 30-day window.'
'X% of accepted requests return a successful response (HTTP 200 with a non-error payload).'
The allowed downtime or error rate per measurement window. For a 99.5% availability SLO over 30 days, the error budget is 0.5% × 30 × 24 × 60 = 216 minutes of downtime.

Claude integrations have an external dependency — the Anthropic API — that the delivery team does not control. Setting a 99.9% availability SLO for an integration that depends on a 99.9% SLA service is optimistic. Model the dependency chain realistically and set the SLO accordingly.

attrs.alt: Availability budget calculation across the dependency chain for a RAG Claude integration

content.text: The maximum achievable availability for the integration is bounded by the product of the availability of its dependencies. For a series-connected dependency chain (each component must be up for the integration to work), the composite availability is the product of individual availabilities.

# nfrs/availability_model.py
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class Dependency:
    name: str
    availability: float       # e.g. 0.999 for 99.9%
    is_critical: bool = True  # if False, integration can degrade without it
    fallback_availability: Optional[float] = None  # if degraded mode exists

def series_availability(dependencies: list[Dependency]) -> float:
    # All critical dependencies must be up
    result = 1.0
    for dep in dependencies:
        if dep.is_critical:
            result *= dep.availability
    return result

def parallel_availability(deps: list[Dependency]) -> float:
    # Integration works if ANY of the parallel deps are up
    # P(all down) = product of (1 - availability)
    p_all_down = 1.0
    for dep in deps:
        p_all_down *= (1 - dep.availability)
    return 1 - p_all_down

# RAG chat integration dependency chain
dependencies = [
    Dependency("Corporate SSO", availability=0.999),
    Dependency("API Gateway", availability=0.9995),
    Dependency("Backend Service", availability=0.999),  # own target
    # Retrieval and Claude are in parallel (degraded mode if retrieval fails)
]
retrieval = Dependency("Vector DB", availability=0.999, is_critical=False)
claude_api = Dependency("Anthropic API", availability=0.999)

# Backend × proxy × SSO (series)
base_availability = series_availability(dependencies)
print(f"Base chain availability: {base_availability:.4%}")

# Composite: base × max(claude, parallel(claude, retrieval))
# Simplified: base × claude_api availability
composite = base_availability * claude_api.availability
print(f"Composite availability: {composite:.4%}")  # ~99.7%

# Recommended SLO: 10% below composite to account for measurement variance
recommended_slo = composite - 0.002  # 99.5%
print(f"Recommended SLO: {recommended_slo:.1%}")
Integration is on the critical path for revenue generation; no fallback process exists
Integration substantially improves a business process; manual fallback exists but is expensive
Integration improves productivity but the underlying process works without it
Nightly or weekly batch jobs; SLO measured as job completion rate, not uptime

Most Claude integrations should start at 99.5% or 99.0%. Committing to 99.9% requires active investment in redundancy, multi-region failover, and 24/7 incident response. Set the SLO you can maintain, not the one that sounds impressive.

An error for the purpose of the reliability SLO is any request that results in an outcome worse than the success definition. The integration must define success precisely — an ambiguous success definition produces an ambiguous error rate.
5xx responses from the integration's API are always errors. 4xx responses from callers (invalid input) are not errors chargeable to the integration.
Responses that are HTTP 200 but contain an error payload (e.g. 'The service is temporarily unavailable') count as errors against the reliability SLO.

Responses that are technically successful (HTTP 200, non-error payload) but fail the integration's quality threshold (e.g. hallucinated, off-topic) are tracked separately as quality metrics, not reliability metrics.
Requests that exceed the P99 latency SLO and are terminated by the integration's timeout mechanism count as errors.
Claude integrations have reliability failure modes that do not exist in traditional software. These require NFRs and operational controls that go beyond standard error rate tracking.

The Anthropic API returns transient errors (429 rate limit, 529 overloaded, 5xx server errors) that are not failures of the integration's own infrastructure. The reliability NFR must specify how these are handled.
Retry with exponential backoff; do not count as an error if resolved within the retry window; alert if rate limit is hit > 5 times per hour
Same as 429; Anthropic-specific status for high server load
Retry up to 4 times; count as integration error only if all retries fail
Count as timeout error; do not retry (indicates slow response, not transient error)
Do not retry; classify as client error; alert if rate > 1% of requests

RAG integrations depend on a knowledge base that must be kept current. A stale knowledge base produces responses that are technically successful but factually incorrect for the integration's domain. This is a reliability failure mode specific to AI workloads.

The knowledge base must reflect the source of truth within T hours of a source update. T is defined per deployment based on how frequently source content changes and the business cost of stale responses.
Track the maximum age of the most recently indexed document in the knowledge base. Alert when this exceeds the staleness NFR.
Alert when the knowledge base sync job fails or runs longer than 2× its expected duration.

A context overflow occurs when the assembled prompt (system prompt + retrieved context + conversation history + user message) exceeds the model's context window. This causes a hard API error (HTTP 400) and counts as a reliability failure. Context overflows are preventable by enforcing the token budget at prompt assembly time.
NFR: Context overflow rate < 0.1% of requests.
Overflows above this rate indicate a prompt assembly bug or an unexpectedly large input distribution.
Token-count the assembled prompt before calling the API; truncate or summarise components that exceed their budget rather than letting the API reject the request.
Any context overflow in production triggers an immediate review — overflows should not happen if the token budget enforcement is working correctly.

# context/overflow_guard.py
import anthropic
from nfrs.latency_budget import ContextBudget, STANDARD_BUDGET

_tokenizer = anthropic.Anthropic()

def count_tokens(text: str, model: str = "claude-sonnet-4-6") -> int:
    # Use Anthropic's token counting endpoint for accuracy
    response = _tokenizer.beta.messages.count_tokens(
        model=model,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": text}],
    )
    return response.input_tokens

def enforce_history_budget(
    history: list[dict],
    budget_tokens: int,
    model: str = "claude-sonnet-4-6",
) -> list[dict]:
    # Truncate oldest turns until history fits within budget
    while history:
        total = sum(
            count_tokens(msg["content"], model) for msg in history
        )
        if total <= budget_tokens:
            break
        history = history[1:]  # remove oldest turn
    return history

def enforce_context_budget(
    retrieved_chunks: list[str],
    budget_tokens: int,
    model: str = "claude-sonnet-4-6",
) -> list[str]:
    retained = []
    used = 0
    for chunk in retrieved_chunks:
        chunk_tokens = count_tokens(chunk, model)
        if used + chunk_tokens > budget_tokens:
            break
        retained.append(chunk)
        used += chunk_tokens
    return retained
The reliability NFR must include an incident severity classification that determines the response time, escalation path, and communication requirements for each class of reliability event.
Integration fully unavailable; SLO breach rate > 5% in 15 minutes
15 minutes to first response; 1 hour to resolution or workaround
On-call engineer + integration lead + operations lead
Error rate 1–5% over 30 minutes; or P99 latency > 3× SLO
30 minutes to first response; 4 hours to resolution
Error rate 0.5–1% over 1 hour; or KB freshness NFR breached
2 hours to first response; next business day resolution
Integration lead notified; on-call engineer assesses
Degraded mode active but within SLO; sync job slow
Automated ticket; integration lead reviews weekly

The Recovery Time Objective (RTO) defines how quickly the integration must recover to full service after an incident. The Recovery Point Objective (RPO) is not typically applicable to Claude integrations (no persistent state that can be lost), but the knowledge base has an effective RPO.
Time from incident declaration to the integration returning to within SLO. Standard requirement: P1 incidents have RTO ≤ 2 hours; P2 incidents have RTO ≤ 8 hours.
Time from sync failure detection to the next successful sync completing. Standard requirement: ≤ 2 × normal sync interval.
Maximum acceptable data loss for the knowledge base if the sync system fails. Typically set equal to the staleness SLO — if content must be ≤ 4 hours old, the RPO for the knowledge base is 4 hours.

The error budget is the amount of downtime or errors the SLO permits. It is not a target — it is the maximum allowed. When the error budget is consumed faster than the measurement period, the team must stop planned changes and focus on reliability work until the budget is replenished.

Reliability and availability NFRs for Claude integrations require explicit modelling of the external dependency chain — particularly the Anthropic API, which the delivery team does not control. Availability SLOs should be set at 10–20% below the theoretical composite availability of the dependency chain, leaving buffer for measurement variance and planned maintenance.

Reliability NFRs must address AI-specific failure modes: transient model API errors, knowledge base staleness, and context window overflow. Each has concrete operational controls — retry policies, freshness monitoring, and token budget enforcement — that make the NFR achievable rather than aspirational. SLO error budget management closes the loop: when the budget burns faster than planned, planned changes stop and reliability work takes priority until the budget is replenished.
